start.title: Asleep Over the Antithesis
start.text:
Theo's nose is approximately two inches from page 47 of *The Sage of Baltimore*. The chapter is titled 'The Weapons of Wit,' and it has been analyzing, with exhausting thoroughness, how H.L. Mencken uses **antithesis** — the pairing of opposite ideas to produce rhetorical electricity. *Democracy is the theory that the common people know what they want, and deserve to get it good and hard.* Mencken's example sits there like a lit match.

Theo's eyelids descend. The words blur. He thinks, half-dreaming: *is that a definition or a verdict?*

He wakes to the clatter of typewriters.

The desk is gone. He is standing in a long, low room hazed with cigar smoke, the air so thick with ink and bourbon it seems almost solid. Men in rolled shirtsleeves hunch over Underwoods, hunting and pecking with two-fingered fury. Through tall windows, the late afternoon sun slants over a Baltimore he doesn't quite recognize — horse carts and Model Ts sharing the cobblestones below.

A voice from the corner desk cuts through the racket. 'You don't look like you belong here.' A barrel-chested man with a round, moonish face and amused eyes behind round glasses peers at Theo over a manuscript. A cigar juts from the corner of his mouth. 'Good. Nobody interesting does.'

choices.text: Walk toward the man at the corner desk

vocabulary.definition: A rhetorical figure that places contrasting ideas in parallel structure to sharpen the contrast. Mencken used it constantly: 'The demagogue always preaches what the people want to hear; the statesman always preaches what they need.'

act0_mencken_intro.title: The Cigar and the Question
act0_mencken_intro.text:
The man at the corner desk is H.L. Mencken — Henry Louis Mencken, the most widely read journalist in America, the Sage of Baltimore, the man Theodore Dreiser called 'the most powerful private citizen in the United States.' He is forty-five years old, in excellent health, and evidently delighted by Theo's bafflement.

'You want to learn to write?' he asks, leaning back in his chair until it groans ominously. 'Or do you want to learn to think? They are not the same thing, boy — though the **booboisie** confuses them daily, as it confuses nearly everything else daily.' He taps ash from his cigar onto a manuscript he is apparently editing with a red pencil. 'Most men who can string sentences together cannot follow a logical argument from here to the door. Most men who can reason cannot write a paragraph that any sane human being would willingly read.'

He fixes Theo with a look of scientific interest, as an entomologist might regard a previously unclassified beetle. 'So which is it? The craft or the thought?'

Theo thinks of his textbook — the chapter on **rhetoric**, on how argument and expression are inseparable. 'Both,' he says.

Mencken grins. It is the grin of a man who has been waiting for that answer. 'Then you'd better follow me. We start at the Mercury.'

choices.text: Follow Mencken to the American Mercury offices
Ask to come along to the political rally he's covering instead

vocabulary.definition: Mencken's coinage combining 'boob' (a fool or dupe) with 'bourgeoisie' (the middle class). The booboisie are the respectable, conformist, churchgoing mass of Americans who, in Mencken's view, mistake comfort for thought and tradition for truth.

The art of effective or persuasive speaking and writing. In classical tradition, rhetoric encompasses invention (finding arguments), arrangement, style, memory, and delivery. Mencken was a master rhetorician who sometimes pretended to despise rhetoric.

act1_mercury.title: The Magazine and the Rapier
act1_mercury.text:
The American Mercury office is on the fourth floor of a building that smells of paste and typewriter ribbon, and Mencken moves through it like a man who owns not just the room but the air inside it. He drops onto a stool at the layout table and seizes a manuscript with his red pencil.

'Here,' he says, circling a paragraph. 'Listen to this sentence: *The great statesman, filled as he is with noble purpose and boundless dedication to the public welfare, strives unceasingly for the betterment of all mankind.* What is wrong with it?'

Theo reads it. 'It's... vague?'

'It is dishonest,' Mencken says. 'Every adjective is a lie in search of a noun. Noble purpose. Boundless dedication. These phrases do not describe anything. They perform virtue.' He slashes the sentence. 'That is **purple prose** — writing that strains so hard to be impressive it loses all meaning. Now, **irony** is different. **Irony** says one thing and means another. **Sarcasm** hits you with a club. I prefer the rapier.' He writes a new sentence in the margin and shows it to Theo: *The great statesman, as usual, knew precisely what the voters wanted and moved heaven and earth to give it to them — which is to say, he promised everything and delivered taxes.*

'You see the difference?' Mencken says. 'The first sentence admires. The second one dissects. That is **satire** — the satirist is a physician who refuses to write pleasant diagnoses.'

choices.text: Ask Mencken to explain the difference between **satire** and mere cruelty
Ask to try writing a sentence yourself, in Mencken's style
Ask about the authors Mencken champions — who is worth reading?

vocabulary.definition: A figure of speech (and a mode of thought) in which the literal meaning differs from — often contradicts — the intended meaning. Irony is subtle; it expects the reader to detect the gap between what is said and what is meant.
A form of verbal irony that is overtly contemptuous or mocking. Where irony invites the reader to think, sarcasm simply insults. Mencken considered sarcasm a blunt instrument.
A literary mode that uses humor, irony, exaggeration, or ridicule to expose and criticize vice or folly — in individuals, institutions, or society at large. Mencken's journalism was sustained satire.
Writing that is overly ornate, flowery, or excessively elaborate — so extravagant in its diction and imagery that it draws attention to itself rather than to its subject.

act1_rally.title: The Science of the Crowd
act1_rally.text:
The rally is in a church hall that smells of hymnbooks and honest sweat. A man at the podium — thick-necked, red-faced, with the voice of a camp revivalist — is explaining to the assembled voters that their problems have a single cause, and that cause has a single face, and that face belongs to Wall Street, which is owned by foreigners, who are in league with the newspapers, which are owned by cowards and liars. The crowd roars.

Mencken sits in the back row, notebook open, pencil moving, wearing the expression of a naturalist who has discovered a new species of flatworm.

'Watch him,' Mencken murmurs. 'Notice the **antithesis**. He puts Good against Evil, America against Its Enemies, the Pure People against the Corrupt Elite. He does this because his audience can hold exactly two ideas at once — and not three. The moment you introduce a third idea, you lose them.' He marks something in his notebook. 'A **demagogue** does not argue. He performs. **Rhetoric** in his hands is not a tool of reason — it is a tool of arousal. And a crowd aroused is a crowd that has stopped thinking.'

Theo watches. The speaker has now moved to banging the podium. The crowd is on its feet. Several people nearby are weeping with conviction.

choices.text: Ask Mencken: is the speaker lying, or does he believe what he's saying?
Ask Mencken how to write about this without simply being contemptuous
Go talk to someone in the crowd — understand the appeal from inside

vocabulary.definition: A rhetorical figure that places contrasting ideas in parallel structure to sharpen the contrast. Mencken used it constantly: 'The demagogue always preaches what the people want to hear; the statesman always preaches what they need.'
A political leader who gains power by appealing to popular emotions, prejudices, and fears rather than by reason or principled argument. From the Greek demos (people) + agogos (leader).

The art of effective or persuasive speaking and writing. In classical tradition, rhetoric encompasses invention (finding arguments), arrangement, style, memory, and delivery. Mencken was a master rhetorician who sometimes pretended to despise rhetoric.

act1_satire_vs_cruelty.title: The Physician's Diagnosis
act1_satire_vs_cruelty.text:
Mencken sets down his red pencil. He has the look of a man who has considered this question before — late at night, perhaps, with better bourbon than the current bottle.

'The difference,' he says, 'is diagnosis versus persecution. A satirist describes what is wrong and why. A bully simply enjoys the wrongness.' He picks up the manuscript again. 'When I write that the average American politician is a coward, a liar, and a fraud, I am not calling him names. I am making a clinical observation supported by two decades of evidence. The evidence is available to anyone who reads a newspaper — which most Americans do not, preferring to read headlines and call it the same thing.'

He writes a sentence, crosses it out, writes another. '**Invective** is the lowest form of **polemic**. I try to avoid it. **Polemic** argues; invective merely shouts. And shouting,' he adds, 'is what the booboisie does when it runs out of arguments, which is almost immediately.'

Theo presses: 'But don't your targets get hurt? Don't ordinary people get caught in the crossfire?'

Mencken pauses. For once, the performance drops slightly. 'The surgeon hurts the patient,' he says. 'That is not an argument against surgery.'

choices.text: Ask to try writing a sentence in Mencken's style
Go with Mencken to meet Theodore Dreiser

vocabulary.definition: A strong verbal or written attack on a person, opinion, or doctrine. A polemic argues aggressively for a position; unlike an essay, it does not pretend to be balanced.
Violent or abusive language directed against a person or cause. Distinguished from polemic in that polemic argues while invective simply attacks.

act1_demagogue_question.title: The Sincere Charlatan
act1_demagogue_question.text:
Mencken considers this with genuine interest. Outside, the rally continues. Someone in the crowd has started singing.

'A subtle question,' he says, 'and the answer is the most dangerous possible: he believes every word.' He closes his notebook. 'The charlatan who knows he is lying can be exposed. The true demagogue — the Bryan type — is impervious to argument because argument assumes good faith, and sincere conviction does not operate in good faith. It operates in revelation.' He tilts his head toward the sound of the singing. 'Those people are not being manipulated. They are being confirmed. There is a great difference.'

Theo thinks of the word his textbook used: **populism** — the appeal to ordinary people against elites. 'But aren't their grievances real?' he asks. 'Isn't there something legitimate in what they want?'

'The grievance may be real,' Mencken says. 'The diagnosis is wrong. And a doctor who makes the wrong diagnosis does not help the patient, regardless of how sincerely he believes in his prescription.' He stands. 'Come. I know a place where nobody sings.'

choices.text: Follow Mencken to the speakeasy he has in mind
Try writing about the rally — make it cut

vocabulary.definition: A political approach that appeals to ordinary people who feel their concerns are disregarded by established elite groups. Mencken was deeply skeptical of populism, seeing it as the channeling of mass resentment into anti-intellectual politics.

act1_crowd.title: The View from Inside
act1_crowd.text:
Theo edges into the crowd and finds himself next to a man about his father's age — worn jacket, careful hair, the look of someone who shines his shoes every Sunday. The man is listening to the speaker with the focused attention of someone hearing the one explanation that finally makes sense.

'You believe him?' Theo asks, carefully.

The man gives him a sidelong look. 'My brother lost his job at the mill six months ago. He's got three kids. You tell me who did that.' He turns back to the speaker. 'At least that man up there says it out loud. The newspapers won't.'

Theo retreats to where Mencken waits, still making notes. He reports what the man said.

Mencken's pencil pauses. 'And?' His tone is non-committal — almost inviting something.

'He had a real problem,' Theo says. 'The speaker gave him a fake answer. But the problem was real.'

'Precisely,' Mencken says, in a tone that sounds almost like approval. 'The **demagogue** is a symptom, not a cause. The disease is a political system that offers no better physician.' He closes his notebook. 'Write that down. It is more important than anything I have said today.'

choices.text: Pursue this line: ask Mencken what a better system would look like
Ask to try writing about the rally

vocabulary.definition: A political leader who gains power by appealing to popular emotions, prejudices, and fears rather than by reason or principled argument. From the Greek demos (people) + agogos (leader).

act1_typewriter.title: One Sentence
act1_typewriter.text:
Mencken commandeers a vacant typewriter and waves Theo into the chair. 'Write one sentence about that politician,' he says, meaning the demagogue at the rally, or the general species thereof. 'One sentence. Make it sing. Make it cut.'

Theo sits. His fingers hover over the keys. He types his first attempt — something straightforward, journalistic, careful. Mencken reads it and says nothing. His expression says everything.

Theo tries again. This time he reaches for **connotation** — he doesn't just describe what the man does, he chooses words that carry judgment in their very sound: not *said* but *proclaimed*; not *claimed* but *testified*. He adjusts the **register** from the neutral to the elevated, then subverts the elevation with a single flat, clinical word at the end.

Mencken reads the second attempt. A long pause. 'Better,' he says. 'The **diction** is working. You have learned something.' He hands the paper back. 'The lesson is this: style is not decoration. Style is argument made palpable. Every word you choose is a vote for how the reader feels. Cast your votes carefully.'

choices.text: Ask about Mencken's battle against literary censorship — who is Dreiser?
Follow Mencken to his next assignment — Prohibition is providing material

vocabulary.definition: Word choice — the specific words a writer selects and why. Good diction is precise, purposeful, and appropriate to the context; it shapes meaning, tone, and emotional effect simultaneously.
The level of formality or informality in language, adjusted for audience, occasion, and purpose. A skilled writer shifts register deliberately — moving from elevated to colloquial to comic effect.

The emotional associations or implications carried by a word, beyond its literal definition (denotation). 'Statesman' and 'politician' denote the same occupation but carry very different connotations.

act1_dreiser.title: The Truth and Its Enemies
act1_dreiser.text:
Theodore Dreiser occupies a study that appears to have been ransacked by someone in a hurry, then partially restored, then ransacked again. He is a large, rumpled man with the eyes of someone who has looked at America for a long time and found it wanting — not angrily, but with the patience of a scientist watching an experiment fail.

Mencken introduces Theo and then, without preamble, launches into an argument with Dreiser about a publisher's cowardice. 'They gutted *Sister Carrie*,' Mencken says. 'They cut the best parts because they were afraid of the ladies' committees. This is what I call **Comstockery** — the organized impulse of the **genteel tradition** to make literature safe, which is to say, to make it useless.'

Dreiser nods slowly. 'They want books to lie,' he says. 'To say that virtue is rewarded and vice is punished and America is just and the poor are poor for their own good. I write about people who are poor and don't deserve it and I am called obscene.'

Mencken turns to Theo. '**Censorship** works by fear. The censors do not ban bad books. They ban true ones. A bad book threatens no one. A true book threatens everyone.' He picks up Dreiser's manuscript and weighs it in his hand. 'This is what literature is for.'

choices.text: Ask what separates honest literature from mere shock value
Ask about the larger culture war — Prohibition, the Scopes Trial, the whole battle

vocabulary.definition: A term coined by philosopher George Santayana for the refined, idealistic, and moralistic strand of American culture that emphasized propriety, optimism, and spirituality — and which Mencken considered a form of organized dishonesty.

Mencken's term (borrowed from Shaw) for the zealous censorship of literature and art on moral grounds, named after Anthony Comstock, the 19th-century anti-vice crusader who used postal laws to suppress books he considered obscene.
The suppression of speech, writing, or other expression deemed objectionable by an authority — political, religious, or social. Mencken fought literary censorship throughout his career.

act2_speakeasy.title: The Puritan's Nightmare
act2_speakeasy.text:
The speakeasy is down a flight of stairs behind a laundry, through a door that opens when you knock in a particular rhythm. Inside, it is warm and loud and smells of beer and sawdust, and Mencken is evidently a regular — the barman has his glass poured before he reaches his stool.

'Prohibition,' Mencken announces, to no one in particular and everyone in general, 'is the Puritan's one great gift to American civilization: the proof that you can make a law that everyone agrees is stupid and still enforce it on the poor while the rich laugh.' He takes a long drink. '**Temperance** was a women's movement, a Midwestern movement, a nativist movement. It was about controlling immigrants who drank beer on Sundays instead of sitting in church. Its victory was the Constitution's greatest embarrassment.'

Theo looks around. The room is full of people who appear perfectly ordinary — salesmen, a judge, what looks like a schoolteacher.

'**Puritanism**,' Mencken continues, lowering his voice to a theatrical whisper, 'is the haunting fear that someone, somewhere, is happy.' He lets the sentence land. 'And **Comstockery** is its enforcement arm — the organized campaign to make sure nobody enjoys anything the Puritan disapproves of. Write that down. I've used it before and I will use it again.'

choices.text: Ask what drives Puritanism — is it really about control?
Ask about the Scopes Trial — Mencken covered it, and it was his finest hour
Push back: isn't temperance a real public health issue, not just prudery?

vocabulary.definition: Originally, the 17th-century English Protestant movement that emphasized strict moral and religious discipline. Mencken used 'Puritan' more broadly to describe the American impulse toward moral regulation and the suppression of pleasure, individualism, and free thought.

The movement to reduce or eliminate the consumption of alcohol, culminating in the Eighteenth Amendment (1919), which established Prohibition. Mencken saw it as a vehicle for rural Protestant culture to impose its values on urban immigrant populations.

Mencken's term (borrowed from Shaw) for the zealous censorship of literature and art on moral grounds, named after Anthony Comstock, the 19th-century anti-vice crusader who used postal laws to suppress books he considered obscene.

act2_puritanism_deeper.title: The Anatomy of Moral Regulation
act2_puritanism_deeper.text:
Mencken sets down his glass and becomes, for a moment, genuinely analytical rather than merely amusing. It is, Theo thinks, a better Mencken — less performer, more thinker.

'Puritanism is not about virtue,' he says. 'It is about power. The man who gets his neighbor's Sunday beer banned has achieved two things: he has demonstrated his own superiority, and he has punished the neighbor for not being him. This is the deep pleasure of **moral regulation** — it converts personal preference into civic obligation. It makes my distaste your crime.'

He traces a ring on the bar with his glass. 'The same mechanism operates in every censorship campaign, every book-burning, every crusade against **obscenity**. The thing being banned is almost never the real target. The real target is difference. Nonconformity. The existence of people who live otherwise and are apparently unashamed.'

Theo thinks about this. 'But don't societies need shared norms? Don't some things need to be regulated?'

Mencken nods, mildly. 'Murder. Theft. Fraud. These are actions that harm others and require restraint. But beer on Sunday? A novel with an unhappy ending? These harm no one and frighten everyone, which is a very different thing.'

choices.text: Ask about the Scopes Trial and fundamentalism
Ask about the booboisie — who exactly is Mencken's real target?

vocabulary.definition: The use of law or social pressure to enforce conformity to a community's moral standards, typically targeting behaviors (drinking, gambling, certain sexual conduct) that the dominant group finds objectionable.

Material deemed offensive to accepted standards of decency or morality. In Mencken's era, 'obscenity' was the legal charge used against books like Dreiser's, even when the real objection was to their realism or social criticism.

act2_pushback_prohibition.title: The Other Side
act2_pushback_prohibition.text:
Theo picks his words carefully. 'I've read about the saloon culture. Women whose husbands drank the rent. Kids going hungry. Isn't there a real problem here that temperance was trying to solve?'

Mencken looks at him with something approaching respect. 'There you go,' he says. 'That is an actual argument, and it deserves an actual answer.' He refills his glass. 'Yes. The saloon was a genuine social evil, especially for working-class families. The temperance advocates were not wholly wrong about that.' He pauses. 'But consider the cure. They banned beer for everyone in the Republic, enriched organized crime, corrupted the police, and made drinking fashionable among people who'd previously not bothered. As medical interventions go, this is roughly equivalent to curing a headache by removing the skull.'

He looks at Theo appraisingly. '**Hyperbole**,' he adds, 'but defensible hyperbole. There is a difference between exaggeration for comic effect and exaggeration for deception. The first is a rhetorical device. The second is a lie.'

choices.text: Follow this to the Scopes Trial — where does Mencken get it most right?
Ask about the booboisie and who exactly Mencken blames

vocabulary.definition: Deliberate exaggeration for rhetorical effect — not meant to be taken literally, but to emphasize a point or create comic or emotional impact. Mencken used hyperbole constantly, always making sure the exaggeration was recognizable as such.

act2_bryan.title: Dayton, Tennessee
act2_bryan.text:
Mencken needs no encouragement on the subject of William Jennings Bryan. He was in Dayton, Tennessee for the Scopes Trial in 1925 — the trial of a schoolteacher for teaching evolution — and he covered it with the ferocity of a man who had been waiting his whole career for exactly this moment.

'Bryan,' he says, 'was the finest **orator** of his generation, and the most dangerous man I have ever observed in American politics — not because he was evil, but because he was sincere.' He leans forward. 'Sincerity, you will discover, is the great camouflage of the demagogue. Bryan believed every word he said. He believed that **fundamentalism** was the protector of ordinary Americans against the arrogance of scientists and professors. And he was not entirely wrong about the arrogance.'

'But you destroyed him in print,' Theo says.

'I did. And I do not entirely repent it. But I sometimes think I missed something — the genuine faith underneath the populism, the real fear of men who could see their world changing and had no tools to understand the change.' Mencken is quiet for a moment. It is a rare silence. 'I am not known for mercy. Perhaps that is a failing.'

choices.text: Ask: can you respect a man and still oppose his ideas?
Ask about the book ban — who is really censoring American thought?

vocabulary.definition: A movement in early 20th-century Protestantism that insisted on the literal truth of the Bible, especially against Darwinian evolution and historical Biblical scholarship. The Scopes Trial (1925) was its greatest public confrontation with secular modernism.

A skilled public speaker. Bryan was famous for his 'Cross of Gold' speech (1896) and his revivalist style on the lecture circuit. Mencken considered oratory a symptom of the democratic disease: the substitution of eloquence for argument.

act2_booboisie.title: A Taxonomy of the Boob
act2_booboisie.text:
Mencken gets out a fresh sheet of paper and draws something — a diagram, almost. At the top, he writes *the superior man*. Below it, a larger circle containing the words *the booboisie*.

'The **booboisie**,' he says, 'is the bourgeoisie of boobs. The respectable, churchgoing, newspaper-reading, flag-waving, conformist mass that administers this Republic. It is not the underclass — the underclass has no power and therefore no importance, except as voters. The **booboisie** is the middle class with pretensions, the class that mistakes **convention** for thought and custom for principle.' He circles the word. 'They are, in a coinage I am particularly pleased with, **Homo boobiens** — man the boob — a creature biologically identical to man the thinker but functionally very different.'

'That's pretty harsh,' Theo says.

'It is precise,' Mencken replies. 'Which in this country passes for harsh.' He puts the paper down. 'The boob is not stupid, exactly. He is incurious. He has stopped asking questions because questions are dangerous and the answers are almost always uncomfortable. He is the natural material of the demagogue, the censor, and the Prohibitionist. Not because he is wicked, but because he has outsourced his conscience to an institution and his mind to a newspaper.'

choices.text: Push back: isn't 'booboisie' just contempt for ordinary people?
Ask where this leads politically — what does democracy do with the booboisie?
Ask about the book ban case Mencken is preparing to fight

vocabulary.definition: Mencken's coinage combining 'boob' (a fool or dupe) with 'bourgeoisie' (the middle class). The booboisie are the respectable, conformist, churchgoing mass of Americans who, in Mencken's view, mistake comfort for thought and tradition for truth.

Mencken's mock-scientific name for the average American conformist — a play on 'Homo sapiens' (thinking man) that substitutes 'boobiens' to suggest a species that has abandoned the defining characteristic of its genus.

A socially established norm, practice, or custom — behavior that is accepted not because it has been reasoned through but because 'everyone does it.' Mencken saw convention as the enemy of genuine thought.

act2_censorship.title: The Book They Banned
act2_censorship.text:
A small crowd of reporters and interested onlookers has assembled outside a bookshop on a Boston street, where a novel has been formally banned on grounds of obscenity. Mencken has a copy under his arm and is selling copies on the sidewalk — deliberately, ostentatiously, daring the police to arrest him, which they do.

He is released three hours later, having filed for the story on the way out.

In the cab back to Baltimore, he is almost cheerful. '**Civil liberties** are not defended by the courts,' he says. 'They are defended by individuals who make the cost of suppression higher than the cost of tolerance.' He shakes out his jacket, which is slightly rumpled from the cell. 'The censors always win in the short term. They have the law. But they always lose in the long term, because they are fighting literature, which has a longer memory than any government.'

Theo has been turning this over. 'What if the book really was obscene?' he asks. 'What if it was genuinely harmful?'

Mencken gives him a level look. 'Name a book that has ever made a decent man wicked. Name one. I will wait.' He settles back. 'Ideas do not corrupt. Stupidity corrupts. Ignorance corrupts. The book merely illuminates what was already there.'

choices.text: Ask: where does Mencken draw the line, if anywhere?
Move to the philosophical core: what does Mencken actually believe about democracy?

vocabulary.definition: The individual rights and freedoms that citizens hold against government interference — freedom of speech, press, religion, assembly, and due process. Mencken was a fierce civil libertarian, defending free expression even for ideas he found repugnant.

act2_elitism_pushback.title: The Uncomfortable Question
act2_elitism_pushback.text:
Theo decides to say the thing that has been forming in his mind for the past several hours. 'I think you're being unfair,' he says. 'The people you call the booboisie — most of them work hard, love their families, and do their best with the information they have. Calling them boobs because they don't read Nietzsche isn't criticism. It's contempt.'

Mencken is quiet for a moment. Not offended — considering. 'Fair enough,' he says, finally. 'There is contempt in it. I will not lie about that. The question is whether the contempt is warranted.' He lights a cigarette. 'You are right that most people work hard and love their families. I do not despise them for that. I despise them — in my harsher moments — for the uses to which they are put by demagogues and Prohibitionists and censors, and for the acquiescence they render.' He pauses. '**Ressentiment** is Nietzsche's word for it — the resentment of the weaker for the stronger, which inverts values and calls mediocrity a virtue. The danger is not that ordinary people exist. The danger is that they are organized against excellence.'

'But YOU get to define excellence,' Theo says. 'Doesn't that make you the tyrant you're warning about?'

choices.text: Press this point — who decides who is superior?
Ask Mencken to explain his actual political philosophy

vocabulary.definition: Nietzsche's term (from French) for the psychology of resentment — specifically, the tendency of those who feel inferior or weak to revalue things so that weakness becomes virtue, excellence becomes vice, and mediocrity is celebrated as righteousness.

act3_democracy.title: Notes on Democracy
act3_democracy.text:
Late afternoon. Mencken's study is a monument to disorder — manuscripts on every surface, books stacked in columns of varying stability, a cat asleep on a pile of proofs. Mencken stands at his standing desk and reads aloud from a manuscript: his new book, *Notes on Democracy*.

'**Democracy**,' he reads, in the tone of a doctor announcing a diagnosis, 'is the theory that the common people know what they want, and deserve to get it good and hard.' He sets the manuscript down. 'That is the first sentence of chapter one. Some of my colleagues find it offensive. I find it accurate.'

Theo looks at the manuscript. 'What's the alternative? **Plutocracy**? **Oligarchy**? Rich men running everything?'

'I am not proposing an alternative,' Mencken says, with a patience that suggests this is a tiresome but necessary clarification. 'I am describing a reality. The physician who tells you that you have a tumor is not thereby proposing cancer as a lifestyle. He is describing what he sees.' He picks up the manuscript again. 'Democracy as practiced in America produces demagogues because demagogues are what democratic voters prefer. It produces **mediocrity** because mediocrity is the democratic ideal: not excellence, but the reassurance that no one is better than anyone else.'

choices.text: Ask about the inferior man — Mencken's most provocative argument
Ask what Mencken actually believes should be done — he must have some prescription
Push back: democracy has real achievements — civil rights, women's suffrage

vocabulary.definition: A system of government in which power is vested in the people, exercised either directly or through elected representatives. Mencken accepted its practical inevitability while denying its philosophical foundations.
Government by the wealthy; a society or system governed by the rich. Mencken did not advocate plutocracy — he despised plutocrats nearly as much as demagogues.
Government by a small group of people, typically an elite defined by wealth, family, military power, or expertise. Mencken's critics accused him of preferring oligarchy; he denied it.
The quality of being average; the absence of distinction. Mencken argued that democracy, by valorizing the average man, institutionalized mediocrity as a civic virtue.

act3_inferior_man.title: The Most Uncomfortable Argument
act3_inferior_man.text:
Mencken reads another passage. 'The **inferior man** — and by this I mean not the poor man, not the uneducated man, but the man constitutionally incapable of abstract thought — is the raw material of **mob rule**. He does not choose to be what he is. He is what nature made him. Democracy's central error is to ask him to do something he cannot do: to evaluate competing political arguments, to resist the appeal of the demagogue, to subordinate his immediate desires to long-term civic good.'

He looks up. 'He cannot do it. Not because he is lazy. Because he is not equipped. And it is unkind — it is, in fact, a kind of cruelty — to build a system that demands this of him and then blames him for failing.'

Theo feels the prose doing something to him — the elevated style, the clinical detachment, the sweeping categorizations. It is seductive. It also makes him uneasy.

'But who decides who is inferior?' he asks. 'You? Your editors at the Mercury? The professors you admire?' He presses the point. 'Isn't this exactly the same move as the demagogue — defining the people you dislike as less than human?'

choices.text: Let Mencken answer — hear the full argument before judging
Push harder — this is the moment to really challenge him
Step back and think about what the prose itself is doing

vocabulary.definition: Mencken's term for the mass of humanity he considered incapable of abstract reasoning — not a moral category but (in his framing) a cognitive one. The concept, drawn partly from Nietzsche, is among his most contested and troubling ideas.

The control of political life by the passions and prejudices of the crowd, typically exploited by demagogues. Mencken saw democratic politics as structurally prone to mob rule whenever a sufficient number of people could be emotionally aroused.

act3_superior_man.title: The Aristocracy of Excellence
act3_superior_man.text:
Mencken answers evenly. 'The **superior man** — again, not a moral category — is the man or woman capable of independent thought, original work, genuine excellence in some field of human endeavor. The scientist who discovers something true. The artist who makes something beautiful. The engineer who makes something that actually works.' He pauses. 'Society produces these people at a low but consistent rate, and then systematically frustrates them in the name of equality.'

'I take Nietzsche's point,' he continues, 'that the **will to power** is not evil — it is the drive toward excellence, toward exceeding oneself, toward making something that did not exist before. Democracy confuses this with arrogance and tries to suppress it. The result is a civilization of the adequate.' He looks at Theo. 'Does that answer your question?'

'It answers it,' Theo says slowly, 'but it leaves a problem. Your superior man is still defined by someone. You can't escape the question of who gets to draw the circle.'

Mencken smiles thinly. 'No,' he says. 'You cannot. That is the problem I describe. I do not pretend to solve it.'

choices.text: Move to Mencken's view of liberty — what does freedom actually mean to him?
Push back one more time on the elitism
Ask about Mencken the man — what does he actually want for America?

vocabulary.definition: In Mencken's usage, a person of genuine intellectual or creative distinction — not defined by class or birth but by the capacity for original thought. He acknowledged the category was problematic but considered it descriptively accurate.

Nietzsche's concept of the fundamental drive in human nature — not toward domination of others (a common misreading) but toward self-mastery, growth, and the expression of one's capacities at their fullest.

act3_liberty.title: What the Average Man Wants
act3_liberty.text:
Mencken stares at the ceiling for a moment. 'The average man,' he says, 'does not want **liberty**. He says he does, in the same way he says he wants justice, truth, and beauty — it is the socially approved thing to say, and he is a social creature above all else. But watch what he does when liberty is actually offered to him.' He picks up his glass. 'He wants someone to tell him what to think. He wants a party, a church, a newspaper, a neighborhood — any **authority** that will relieve him of the burden of judgment. **Conformity** is not the failure of democracy. It is its foundation.'

Theo considers this. There is something true in it, and something that bothers him — the way the argument slides from description to contempt without quite acknowledging the move.

'What do YOU want?' Theo asks. 'Not as a philosopher — as a person living in this country. What do you actually want it to become?'

Mencken looks at him with something that might be surprise. It is not a question he is often asked, apparently. He opens his mouth, closes it again, and says: 'Come to my study tonight. Late. There is a different conversation to be had after midnight.'

choices.text: Go to Mencken's study that night

vocabulary.definition: Freedom from external constraint — political, religious, or social. Mencken was a genuine libertarian in the classical sense: he believed government should leave people alone to make their own choices and bear the consequences.

The adjustment of individual beliefs and behaviors to match group norms. Mencken saw conformity as the dominant American value, enforced not by law but by social pressure, the desire for approval, and the fear of standing out.

The power or right to give orders, make decisions, and enforce obedience. Mencken was deeply skeptical of all institutional authority — governmental, religious, academic — and considered deference to authority the intellectual vice most damaging to American life.

act3_craft_observation.title: The Rhetoric of Contempt
act3_craft_observation.text:
Theo steps back — mentally, at least. He has been so caught up in arguing with the content of Mencken's ideas that he has stopped watching the form.

He looks again at the passage about the inferior man. The Latin-derived vocabulary ('constitutionally incapable,' 'abstract thought') gives it the sound of a scientific report. The **periodic sentence** structure — building to a conclusion — makes each paragraph feel inevitable. The clinical tone doesn't argue; it declares, in a register that implies the debate is already over.

This is a kind of **ethos** argument, he realizes. Mencken is not just stating a position — he is performing a kind of intelligence, a hauteur that says: *I see more clearly than you, and my prose style is the proof.* The style does the arguing.

'Your prose style,' Theo says, 'is doing something your argument doesn't say explicitly. It's asking me to identify with the superior man just by being smart enough to enjoy the writing.'

Mencken stares at him. Then, slowly, he laughs. 'Well done,' he says. 'You have read me more carefully than most of my critics. Come tonight to my study — I want to continue this.'

choices.text: Go to Mencken's study that night

vocabulary.definition: A sentence in which the main clause is withheld until the end, building suspense and creating a sense of inevitability when the conclusion finally arrives. A favorite of orators and polemicists.

One of Aristotle's three modes of persuasion: the appeal to the speaker's credibility, character, and authority. Establishing ethos convinces an audience that you are trustworthy and worth listening to.

act3_pushback.title: The Mirror Question
act3_pushback.text:
Theo has had enough. He presses the full weight of the objection: 'You say democracy fails because the common man can't think. But who told you that? What method did you use? You're a journalist with opinions, not a scientist with data. And your opinions happen to place you — the man with the pen and the magazine and the audience of admirers — in the category of the superior man. Isn't that a little convenient?'

A long pause. The cigar burns.

'Convenient,' Mencken repeats. 'Yes. It is convenient. You are right to notice.' He does not sound defensive — he sounds interested. 'Every **polemicist** exempts himself from his own critique. That is the occupational hazard of invective: the hammer never feels the nail's perspective. I have written against demagoguery while practicing a refined version of the same art.' He stands. 'The question you are asking is the one I most avoid. And avoidance,' he adds, with the ghost of a smile, 'is not the same as ignorance. It is a choice.'

'So what does that make you?' Theo asks.

'A diagnostician,' Mencken says. 'Who has not been tested by the disease he diagnoses. Come to my study tonight. We will see what survives the hour.'

choices.text: Go to Mencken's study that night

vocabulary.definition: A person who engages in polemics — strongly worded, argumentative writing or speech that attacks a position, institution, or person. Mencken was the most celebrated American polemicist of his era.

act4_study.title: After Midnight
act4_study.text:
Mencken's study at night is a different country. The performance is gone. He sits in a worn leather chair, without his jacket, a glass on the side table and a book open on his lap — a volume of Nietzsche, dog-eared to ruin. The cigar is out.

'I am not a political philosopher,' he says, without preamble. 'I have never pretended to be. A political philosopher proposes a system. I am a **diagnostician**: I describe the disease. I do not prescribe the cure, because I do not believe I know the cure, and I have contempt for those who claim otherwise.' He closes the book. 'What I know is that rhetoric is the medium in which political life swims, and that most people cannot tell a good argument from a performance of conviction. This is dangerous. I try to make it funny, because if I did not make it funny I would become either a demagogue or a drunk.'

He looks at Theo with genuine attention for the first time. 'You have been pushing back at me all day. Good. That is the right response. The wrong response is to swallow me whole.' He pauses. 'What have you actually learned? Not what you've decided to believe — what have you learned to *do*?'

choices.text: I've learned to watch how prose argues — style as strategy
I've learned to think for myself, including against Mencken
I've learned that the enemies of free thought are real and Mencken is right to fight them
I want to write — to be a journalist, to tell the truth with style
Mencken is right about almost everything — I agree with him completely
Honestly? I found him insufferable. Brilliant but insufferable.
I learned that most people are fools and institutions are corrupt

vocabulary.definition: One who identifies the nature of a problem or disease through observation and analysis. Mencken's self-description as a diagnostician rather than a reformer was his way of claiming honesty without claiming authority.

act4_craft_path.title: The Weapon and Its Use
act4_craft_path.text:
Mencken nods slowly. 'Then you have learned the most important thing,' he says. 'Not what to think, but how writing thinks for you — or against you, if you are not careful.'

He stands and goes to his desk. He picks up a single typed page and hands it to Theo. It is a paragraph from *Notes on Democracy* — one of the moderate ones, the analytical ones, where Mencken is more physician than entertainer.

'Read it as a rhetorical object,' he says. 'Not as argument. As artifact. Notice the **syntax** — how the sentences are structured to feel inevitable. Notice the diction — how the choices of word carry connotation before the argument even begins. Notice the **rhythm** — how the long periodic sentences build energy that the short gut-punch releases.'

Theo reads. And reads again. And for the first time all day, he sees it whole: the craft and the content, the rapier and the hand that holds it. Rhetoric is not decoration. It is *argument made palpable*.

The cigar smoke thins. The study blurs. The words on the page shimmer and reassemble themselves as something else — a sentence, in his own voice, that he has been trying to write all day. He reaches for the pencil on Mencken's desk.

He wakes at his own desk. The textbook is open to chapter four. His hand is moving.

choices.text: Finish the journey

vocabulary.definition: The arrangement of words and phrases to create well-formed sentences. Syntactic choices — sentence length, word order, use of subordinate clauses — shape the rhythm and emphasis of prose as powerfully as word choice.

The pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables and long and short sentences in prose. Good rhythmic control makes writing feel inevitable and memorable; poor rhythm makes it feel clumsy or monotonous.

act4_independent_path.title: The Student Who Disagreed
act4_independent_path.text:
Mencken looks at him for a long moment. Then he does something Theo has not seen him do all day: he laughs — a real laugh, with warmth in it.

'Good,' he says. 'If you agreed with everything I said, you haven't understood me. A man who agrees with everything he reads has not thought at all — he has merely changed the object of his deference.' He settles back. 'The lesson of rhetoric — the real lesson, the one the textbooks bury under diagrams of **logos**, **pathos**, and ethos — is that every act of persuasion is an act of aggression. It is an attempt to move someone from where they are to where you want them to be. The defense is not to become impervious to argument. The defense is to notice when it is happening and to ask, always: *who benefits from my believing this?*'

The study softens at the edges. Outside, Baltimore continues its nocturnal business — distant music, a trolley, the smell of rain on stone.

'You fought me today,' Mencken says. 'That is the only homage worth paying to a thinker. Not agreement. Engagement.' He raises his glass. 'Go home and write something I would disagree with. That will be the beginning of your education.'

choices.text: Finish the journey

vocabulary.definition: One of Aristotle's three modes of persuasion: the appeal to logic, reason, and evidence. A logos-based argument demonstrates its claims through facts, statistics, and logical reasoning.
One of Aristotle's three modes of persuasion: the appeal to emotion. A pathos-based argument moves its audience through stories, vivid imagery, or appeals to values they hold dear.

act4_admirer_path.title: The Open Door
act4_admirer_path.text:
Mencken accepts this with a nod. 'Then you have found your way into a library that will keep you busy for years,' he says. 'Not just me — Nietzsche, Swift, Shaw, Twain, Bierce. The whole tradition of intelligent contempt, deployed in the service of intelligence.' He hands Theo a list — titles, names, a brief annotation on each. 'Read these. Argue with them. When you find yourself agreeing too easily, slow down — that is where the manipulation lives.'

There is warmth in the room now, or something like it — the warmth of a shared enthusiasm, a reader recognizing another reader. 'I do not need disciples,' Mencken says. 'I need readers. A disciple carries your water. A reader carries your argument into places you cannot go.' He stands. 'Read **Swift**. Read **Voltaire**. They did this before I did it, and they did it better, and their targets are still with us.' He gestures toward the window, toward Baltimore, toward America. 'The disease is perennial. We are not.'

choices.text: Finish the journey

vocabulary.definition: Jonathan Swift (1667-1745), Anglo-Irish satirist, author of Gulliver's Travels and A Modest Proposal. For Mencken, Swift represented the gold standard of satirical prose: savage, precise, and wickedly funny.
Francois-Marie Arouet (1694-1778), French Enlightenment writer whose wit and satirical attacks on the Church, superstition, and tyranny made him Mencken's primary European predecessor.

act4_journalist_path.title: The Craft and the Calling
act4_journalist_path.text:
Mencken looks at him steadily. 'Then you have understood the best part of what I do and ignored the worst,' he says. 'Good. The journalism is better than the philosophy.' He turns to his desk and pulls out a single typed sentence — apparently a practice piece, the kind he produces before writing anything important.

'The journalist's job,' he says, 'is not to be neutral. It is to be accurate. **Neutrality** is the coward's substitute for honesty — it pretends that truth has two equal sides and that the journalist's job is to stand equidistant from both.' He hands Theo the paper. 'Read that sentence. Tell me what it does.'

The sentence is simple, direct, and cuts like a freshly stropped blade. Every word is chosen; there is no fat. The diction is elevated just enough to give the whole thing authority, then undercut at the last word with something concrete and slightly undignified — the Mencken signature.

'It tells the truth,' Theo says. 'With style.'

'That,' Mencken says, 'is journalism.' He sits back. 'Now go write one of your own, and do not stop until it is honest.'

choices.text: Finish the journey

vocabulary.definition: The journalistic principle (or pretense) of presenting all sides equally without taking a position. Mencken rejected it as a form of intellectual cowardice — the refusal to make a judgment even when judgment is clearly warranted.

act4_disciple_path.title: The Perfect Student
act4_disciple_path.text:
All day, Theo has been nodding. Everything Mencken says is brilliant, and Theo has been smart enough to see its brilliance, and hasn't that been a kind of pleasure — the pleasure of being the one person in the room who understands? The inferior man, the booboisie, the demagogue — yes, yes, yes. Theo has seen them all. He is not one of them.

Now, late in the study, Mencken asks what Theo has learned, and Theo says: 'That you're right about almost everything.'

A long silence. The cigar smoke drifts.

'If you agree with everything I say,' Mencken says at last, 'you haven't understood me.' His voice is flat — not unkind, but flat. 'I am a polemicist. My job is to provoke thought, not to replace it. A man who swallows me whole has merely changed his authority — he has traded the booboisie's newspaper for my magazine, and called it liberation.' He stands. 'Go home. Read someone who disagrees with me. Read the people I am wrong about. Then come back.' He pauses at the door. 'If you come back.'

choices.text: Finish the journey

act4_offended_path.title: The Fireworks and the View
act4_offended_path.text:
Theo has been getting angrier all day. The contempt for ordinary people. The certainty. The performance of superiority dressed up as clear thinking. Now, in the study, Mencken asks what Theo has learned, and Theo says: 'That you're brilliant and you know it and you enjoy it too much.'

Mencken accepts this like a man who has heard it before. 'Fair,' he says. 'I do enjoy it. Guilty as charged.' He does not apologize. 'But tell me: have you thought about whether I am *right*? Not whether I am pleasant, not whether I am humble — whether the arguments hold.' He looks at Theo steadily. 'You have spent the day disliking me. You have not spent the day disagreeing with me. These are different activities.'

Theo opens his mouth. Closes it.

'The **ad hominem**,' Mencken says gently, 'is the last refuge of the man who has run out of arguments. I am arrogant. That is true. It does not follow that I am wrong.' He gestures toward the door. 'Go home. Separate the question of whether you like me from the question of whether I am right. You may be surprised at what you find.'

choices.text: Finish the journey

vocabulary.definition: A logical fallacy that attacks the person making an argument rather than the argument itself. Latin for 'to the person.' Mencken was often attacked ad hominem, and was sometimes guilty of the same.

act4_cynical_path.title: The Sneer Without the Craft
act4_cynical_path.text:
By evening, Theo has absorbed the posture without the discipline. He has the vocabulary — booboisie, demagogue, inferior man — and the tone: the weary certainty that most people are foolish and most institutions are corrupt. It is very satisfying, this tone. It feels like wisdom.

Mencken watches him hold forth at the dinner table — deploying the terms, landing the contempt — and says nothing for a while. Then, quietly: 'What did you write today?'

Theo blinks. 'I... we were talking.'

'Yes,' Mencken says. 'We were talking. You absorbed a register and a set of connotations and a habit of dismissal. You did not write a sentence.' He stubs out his cigar. 'Contempt is easy. Any fool can sneer. The hard part — the only part that matters — is making the contempt earn its keep by illuminating something true.' He stands. 'You got the worst of me and none of the best. That is a bad trade.'

choices.text: Finish the journey

end_craftsman.title: The Sentence That Cuts
end_craftsman.text:
Theo wakes at his desk, cheek pressed against page 47 of *The Sage of Baltimore*. His hand is moving — he is writing in the margin, a sentence he somehow knows, a sentence he has not written before but that arrived complete.

He reads it. It is not Mencken's sentence. It is his own — in his own voice, his own diction, aimed at his own target. But it uses everything Mencken showed him: the antithesis that makes the reader feel the gap between what is claimed and what is true; the connotation that does the work before the argument begins; the short final clause that lands like a stone in water.

He has learned to separate the craft from the content. He can admire Mencken's prose and question his conclusions. He understands, now, why his textbook spent a chapter on antithesis — not because antithesis is decorative, but because antithesis is *thinking made visible*. Every rhetorical figure is a way of organizing perception, of forcing the mind to see something it would otherwise slide past.

He reads his sentence again. It sings. It cuts.

He reaches for a fresh sheet of paper.

end_independent.title: The Better Argument
end_independent.text:
Theo wakes at his desk. The textbook is open, but he is not reading it — he is writing on a separate sheet, an argument against something Mencken said. He cannot remember exactly when he started, or whether he was awake or asleep when the argument took its current shape.

It is not a long argument. Three paragraphs. But it is his — genuinely his, not a summary of anyone else's position, not a repositioning within a pre-set debate. It takes Mencken's diagnostic method — clear-eyed, precise, willing to say uncomfortable things — and turns it on Mencken himself.

The lesson was never the content. The lesson was the *practice of independent thought* — the willingness to engage any idea, including ideas you find attractive, with the same critical tools you'd apply to ideas you distrust. A good teacher does not produce disciples. A good teacher produces students who can eventually correct the teacher.

He writes one more line: *The defense against a brilliant rhetorician is not to refuse to read him. It is to read him so carefully that you know exactly where he is wrong.*

He caps his pen. That will do.

end_admirer.title: A Door Opens
end_admirer.text:
Theo wakes at his desk, and the first thing he does is reach for his phone to search for something Mencken said that he cannot quite remember. The exact phrase. The exact cadence.

He finds it. And then he finds a review of *Notes on Democracy*, written by someone who disagrees with Mencken, and it is good — sharp, fair, landing blows Theo recognizes as legitimate. He reads it twice.

He is not a convert, exactly. He is something better: a reader who has found a writer worth arguing with. The list Mencken gave him — Swift, Voltaire, Twain, Shaw — he will work through it. He will read the people who disagree, too. He will find out who is right.

A door has opened. Not onto certainty, but onto a conversation — old, ongoing, and evidently not finished. He has joined it at an interesting moment.

He writes Mencken's sentence in his notebook: *Democracy is the theory that the common people know what they want, and deserve to get it good and hard.* Below it he writes a question mark.

end_journalist.title: The Truth With Style
end_journalist.text:
Theo wakes at his desk and opens a new document.

He has covered a rally before — a school board meeting, a local election — and he wrote about it the way he was taught: balanced, neutral, *he said / she said*, both sides, no judgment. He reads those pieces now and they are accurate and lifeless. They describe what happened without illuminating what it meant.

He writes a new lede. It is opinionated. It takes a position. It uses connotation deliberately, chooses its verbs for their punch, ends with a short sentence that lands the point without announcing it. It tells the truth — verifiable, fact-checked truth — but it tells it *with style*, which is to say, it tells it in a way that makes you feel what it means to know it.

He will be a journalist. A good one. He will tell the truth, and he will not be neutral about the truth — because neutrality, Mencken was right about this much, is not a virtue. It is an evasion.

He saves the document. He hits send.

end_disciple.title: The Borrowed Voice
end_disciple.text:
Theo wakes at his desk and writes three paragraphs in a voice that is not quite his own. The sentences have the Mencken rhythm — the long build, the short gut-punch — and the Mencken vocabulary, and the Mencken targets: the booboisie, the demagogue, the Puritan. It is good writing, in the sense that the mechanics work. It is not his writing.

He has traded one authority for another. He has replaced the conformity of the crowd with the conformity of the iconoclast — which is still conformity, just with better prose style.

Mencken's voice in the back of his mind: *If you agree with everything I say, you haven't understood me.* He pushes it away.

Somewhere, at the end of a path not taken, the real lesson waits — the one about reading against the grain, about separating the instrument from the hand, about finding his own voice by learning to resist the ones that sound most like wisdom. He has not taken that path today.

There is time. But he should hurry.

end_offended.title: The Fireworks
end_offended.text:
Theo wakes at his desk and his first feeling is indignation, well-ripened and satisfying. Mencken was arrogant. Mencken was contemptuous. Mencken called ordinary people boobs and felt very clever about it. These are real failings, and Theo has catalogued them with care.

He has also missed everything else.

He has not noticed how the antithesis works, or what the clinical register does to an argument's credibility, or how the short final sentence lands in a way that a longer one cannot. He has not thought about the distinction between irony and sarcasm, or whether *Notes on Democracy* is right or wrong about the things it describes, or what Mencken's method — divorced from his conclusions — might teach him.

He judged the man and missed the prose. The fireworks were spectacular and they blocked the view.

His textbook is still open to page 47. He should probably read it.

end_cynical.title: The Sneer
end_cynical.text:
Theo wakes at his desk with a new vocabulary and a new tone. He can describe the booboisie now, and the demagogue, and the Puritan, and the inferior man. He can deploy these terms with a casual fluency that sounds, to his own ear, like wisdom.

He has not written anything.

He has the contempt without the craft — the dismissal without the diagnosis, the sneer without the sentence that earns the sneer. Mencken's method was never the contempt; the contempt was the byproduct, the smoke from the engine. The engine was precision: exact diction, disciplined syntax, the willingness to be specific where it would have been easier to be vague.

Theo can sneer. He cannot yet write.

This is the worst trade available: he got Mencken's worst quality and none of his best. The cure is simple and difficult in equal measure: he must write one true sentence, and not stop until it is honest.
